chapters.title: Every image records how someone once saw something
chapters.text:
Art history chronicles what cultures chose to make visible. Early painting preserved appearances and summoned absent subjects — a king's portrait standing in for the king, a sacred scene instructing illiterate viewers. Masterworks endure because their makers possessed distinctive ways of seeing that still speak across centuries.

The more imaginative the work, the more profoundly it allows us to share the artist's experience of the visible. ~ John Berger

Artistic vision is never purely personal. Era, religion, intended function, and available technology all shape it. Renaissance perspective concentrated the world in a single viewing point — linked to Christianity as God's organizing gaze. As religious authority waned, that unified viewpoint collapsed. Photography proved there is no natural center, and avant-garde movements — impressionism, cubism, expressionism — absorbed the lesson immediately.

Art mirrors its moment: each canvas reveals how one maker once perceived the world.

This summary develops four arguments from the book:

• Reproduction shattered the uniqueness of painted images.
• European oil painting distinguishes nude from naked — linguistically similar, conceptually opposed.
• Oil painting served owners who wanted art to display purchasing power.
• Advertising images now perform that function in public space, offering consumers a new self-image through purchase.

Berger's television series and book asked viewers to notice the assumptions embedded in looking — who controls the frame, who is observed, and who profits from the gaze.

chapters.title: Reproduction detached meaning from the original's location
chapters.text:
Before the nineteenth century, a painting belonged to the place that housed it. Dutch still lifes of the 1600s hung in rooms designed for conversation — the works sparked discussion. Encountering them in a museum or on a screen rewires that social function entirely.

When paintings were created as ordered, they were destined for specific places. The place determined the meaning of the painting.

Photographic reproduction multiplies contexts infinitely. A Mona Lisa postcard on a mood board evokes Parisian memory rather than Leonardo's portrait commission. Paintings become contemporary whenever they surface on television, clothing, or stationery — ephemeral, ubiquitous, democratic for the first time.

The original once carried a single meaning; now value rests in rarity and authenticity rather than narrative content. Price tracks scarcity.

Reproduction fatigue dulls encounters with originals we have already scrolled past on phones. Museums treat masterpieces as relics proving existence while publishing provenance — commissioners, owners, dates — that authenticates the object without explaining what it depicts.

Catalog entries for works like Leonardo's Virgin of the Rocks document ownership chains and creation timelines, certifying uniqueness more than interpreting imagery.

chapters.title: Context rewrites every reproduced image
chapters.text:
In galleries, paintings hang among autonomous peers. In reproduction, they sit beside captions, advertisements, or unrelated pictures — and neighboring material reshapes meaning.

Imagine Van Gogh's cornfield with departing birds in a museum: you like it, ignore it, or muse on private associations.

Open the same image in a biography captioned this is the lost picture Van Gogh painted before he killed himself, and separation becomes impossible. The text colonizes the image; illustration subordinates painting.

The meaning of an image is changed according to what one sees immediately beside it or what comes immediately after it. ~ John Berger

Reproduction demystified art. Cropped details detach from whole compositions — a beautiful face might belong to an allegorical figure of Justice you never see. Anyone can embed paintings in new arguments.

Thus every work can stay contemporary while its story continues through each new use — political, commercial, personal, or academic. Berger's own book demonstrates the point: reproduced paintings share nothing except the commentary binding them.

Did you know? The Mona Lisa by Leonardo da Vinci became an incredibly famous painting thanks to reproductions. When stolen in 1911, newspapers all over the world ran headlines about the missing masterpiece.

chapters.title: Naked versus nude in the European oil tradition
chapters.text:
European painting's first nudity appears in Eden: Adam and Eve unaware of exposure until the apple awakens mutual scrutiny. Nakedness, Berger argues, exists in the viewer's perception.

That logic persisted into the twentieth century — a painted woman is a woman conscious of being seen unclothed. Naked means simply oneself; nude means displayed for a spectator. Feminist critics including Laura Mulvey extended Berger's framework to cinema's male gaze.

Up to the 20th century, almost all European oil paintings of female nudity were made by men and for men.

Non-European traditions often depict lovers with mutual agency rather than a woman posing for an absent owner.

In European scenes of intimacy, women frequently avert their eyes, performing disinterest while the viewer occupies center stage — a pose advertising and film still recycle.

Since the Renaissance, sexual imagery typically adopts a frontal viewpoint because the spectator-owner stands as protagonist. Exceptions appear when artists paint wives or beloved models: intention, emotion, and relationship surface, and viewers recognize themselves as outsiders. Only then is nakedness non-anonymous.

Did you know? The National Gallery (London), spanning over 2,300 paintings from the 13th to the early 20th century, has only 21 paintings created by female artists.

chapters.title: Owning a painting meant owning the gaze upon its subject
chapters.text:
European oil painting between the 1500s and 1900s served wealthy patrons who treated canvases as celebrations of private property.

Collectors bought culture for love of art and for possession of unique objects. Genre paintings even depicted collectors amid their holdings — a portrait of assets at a moment in time. The medium displayed economic power.

The oil painting in its traditional period was a visualization of what could be bought or possessed by a wealthy man.

Subjects demonstrated owner value rather than intrinsic beauty:

• Family portraits proved lineage.
• Animal paintings signaled livestock worth and status.
• Architectural views asserted land ownership.

Even mythological scenes conveyed moral capital — owning a tableau of passion, death, or heroism suggested how elevated moments ought to be witnessed.

Oil paint's tactile rendering made depicted objects feel physically present — texture, weight, and solidity convincing enough to touch.

chapters.title: Advertising images inherit the oil painting's persuasive logic
chapters.text:
Berger groups billboards, magazine spreads, banners, and promotional material as publicity images — a constant visual environment modern humans navigate without conscious effort.

We accept the system of publicity images as a regular part of the world, as a phenomenon of nature.

These images promise self-transformation through purchase: they borrow your self-regard and sell it back attached to a product. Fitness clubs and cosmetics ads show people already upgraded, inviting comparison.

Unlike oil paintings celebrating what is already possessed, publicity sells becoming rather than having.

Anonymity amplifies power — impersonal faces enlarge illusion. The nude woman's unfocused gaze in old master paintings returns here: seeing is choosing, and choosing confers dominance. A model who avoids eye contact lets the viewer insert himself into the fantasy.

Ads quote oil-painting composition — romantic nature, luxury materials, courtly poses — swapping knights for motorists and Madonnas for mothers selecting baby formula.

Cheap color photography assumed oil paint's documentary role in the twentieth century, freeing painters to create rather than imitate surfaces.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Looking built visual culture. Every image — painted or photographed — encodes one person's way of seeing. Yet creation is not the end: each subsequent viewer brings context that alters meaning, and reproduction multiplies that effect.

This summary focused on European oil painting. Examining key works clarifies Berger's arguments:

• The Venus of Urbino by Titian

Venus faces the viewer, touching herself — clearly engineered to provoke erotic association.

• Hen Pelsken by Peter Paul Rubens

A loved woman's portrait invites no spectator participation; it records intimacy between artist and model.

• The Ambassadors by Hans Holbein the Younger

Status objects demand to be seen; meticulous detail lets modern viewers imagine sixteenth-century possessions.

• Picture gallery of carinal Valenti Gonzaga by Panini

A private collection immortalized on canvas — the Instagram grid of its era.

Art is human-made sight preserved in pigment. Viewers from the 1700s shared more with us than we assume; meet their gaze directly rather than treating canvases as mere historical containers.

Try this

1. Think if you have some reproduction of artwork that has a special meaning to you. And then search for the original purpose of the work.
2. Have a look at the four paintings described in the conclusion.
3. Compare Beyoncé's music video for the song Mine and the Pieta by Michelangelo. Could you notice how similar they are? Why do you think it is like that?
